attributes.stem:
A 16-year-old boy presents to the General Practice clinic with a two-month history of persistent left knee pain. He plays basketball competitively for his high school team and the pain now impairs his ability to play on the court. This pain worsens with physical activity, especially when he jumps up to catch the basketball. The pain has become worse over the past few weeks and at times, he needs to sit for long periods to relieve the pain. He is worried that he can no longer play basketball. There is no history of past trauma or injury. He has no significant medical history and is currently taking ibuprofen for the knee pain. He has no significant family history.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. His BMI is 20 kg/m². There is no swelling or redness surrounding the knee. There is tenderness on the anterior knee that worsens with knee extension against resistance.

An X-ray of the knee is performed (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
Osgood-Schlatter disease (or traction apophysitis) is a tibial osteochondritis at the tibial insertion of the patella tendon. It is thought to be due to the overuse of quadriceps muscle during growth in teenagers or children. It is more common in boys than in girls. The overuse of the quadriceps, especially during physical activities involving jumping or sprinting, during ossification periods in a child may cause repeated avulsion of the patellar ligament on the tibial tuberosity. This results in inflammation or irritation at the apophysis.

Clinical features involve anterior knee pain that worsens with physical activity, commonly involving knee extensions such as jumping or lunges. There may be proximal tibial swelling involved as well. X-rays are diagnostic for traction apophysitis which would reveal anterior soft tissue swelling and fragmentations of the tubercle. Treatment involves non-operative management such as resting, ice and NSAIDs for pain relief. Cast and immobilisation for six weeks or ossicle excision may be necessary if there are severe symptoms involved. This disease may be self-limiting but may not resolve until bone growth has halted.

Patellar tendonitis is also common in jumping athletes. Symptoms may include anterior knee pain at the inferior border of the patellar and pain with prolonged flexion. X-ray findings are usually normal.

Patella bursitis is common in patients who kneel often. Symptoms may include generalised pain and swelling in the knee, which can be red or warm to touch.

Osteochondroma of the proximal tibia is a common benign primary bone tumour. It is usually asymptomatic but can be painful and palpable near the ends of the long bones. X-ray findings include pedunculated or sessile lesions.

Tibial tubercle fracture is an uncommon injury that typically occurs in adolescent athletes during activities involving sudden contraction of the quadriceps muscle, such as jumping or sprinting. Unlike Osgood-Schlatter disease, which develops gradually, a tibial tubercle fracture presents with acute onset of severe pain and inability to bear weight. X-ray findings would show a clear fracture line through the tibial tubercle, often with displacement, which is not evident in this case. The chronic nature of symptoms and the X-ray findings are more consistent with Osgood-Schlatter disease than a tibial tubercle fracture.
learningPoints: Traction apophysitis is an important cause of chronic knee pain in adolescents.
Osgood-Schlatter disease typically presents with anterior knee pain that worsens with activity, especially jumping or running.
X-ray findings in Osgood-Schlatter disease include soft tissue swelling and fragmentation of the tibial tubercle.
references: Stanitski CL. Knee overuse disorders in the pediatric and adolescent athlete. Instr Course Lect 1993; 42:483.
Kujala UM, Kvist M, Heinonen O. Osgood-Schlatter's disease in adolescent athletes. Retrospective study of incidence and duration. Am J Sports Med 1985; 13:236.
Achar S. Apophysitis and Osteochondrosis: Common Causes of Pain in Growing Bones. Am Fam Physician 2019; 99:610-18.
